question:
2. Answer both (i) and (ii). Test for convergence the series
(i) \( \frac{1}{\log (2!)}+\frac{1}{\log (3!)}+\frac{1}{\log (4!)}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\log (n!)}+\cdots \)
(ii) \( \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3} \cdots \sqrt[n]{3}}+\cdots \).
solution:
Solution. For \( n \geq 2 \), we have \( n^{n}>n! \), hence \( n \log n>\log (n!) \) and
\[
\frac{1}{\log (n!)}>\frac{1}{n \log n}
\]

Series (i) therefore dominates the series
\[
\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n \log n}
\]

Since
\[
\int_{2}^{x} \frac{d t}{t \log t}=\log \log x-\log \log 2
\]
the improper integral \( \int_{2}^{\infty} d t /(t \log t) \) diverges, and hence, by the integral test, so does (1). Therefore series (i) is divergent.

The denominator of the \( n \)th term of series (ii) is \( 3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / n)} \), and \( 1+\frac{1}{2} \) \( +\cdots+(1 / n) \sim \log n \). Hence the \( n \)th term of (ii) is about
\[
\frac{1}{3^{\log n}}=\frac{1}{n^{\log 3}}
\]

Now \( \Sigma n^{-p} \) converges if \( p>1 \) and \( \log 3>1 \), so series (ii) converges.
We shall give the details of this argument. Since
\[
\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{1}{k}>\sum_{k=1}^{n} \int_{k}^{k+1} \frac{d t}{t}=\int_{1}^{\dot{n}+1} \frac{d t}{t}=\log (n+1)>\log n
\]
we have
\[
3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / n)}>3^{\log n}=n^{\log 3}
\]

Hence series (ii) is dominated by \( \Sigma n^{-\log 3} \). Since the latter converges, so does (ii).

descriptive_long.question:
2. Answer both (i) and (ii). Test for convergence the series
(i) \( \frac{1}{\log (2!)}+\frac{1}{\log (3!)}+\frac{1}{\log (4!)}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\log (indexvar!)}+\cdots \)
(ii) \( \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3} \cdots \sqrt[indexvar]{3}}+\cdots \).
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. For \( indexvar \geq 2 \), we have \( indexvar^{indexvar}>indexvar! \), hence \( indexvar \log indexvar>\log (indexvar!) \) and
\[
\frac{1}{\log (indexvar!)}>\frac{1}{indexvar \log indexvar}
\]

Series (i) therefore dominates the series
\[
\sum_{indexvar=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{indexvar \log indexvar}
\]

Since
\[
\int_{2}^{upperlimit} \frac{d integrand}{integrand \log integrand}=\log \log upperlimit-\log \log 2
\]
the improper integral \( \int_{2}^{\infty} d integrand /(integrand \log integrand) \) diverges, and hence, by the integral test, so does (1). Therefore series (i) is divergent.

The denominator of the \( indexvar \)th term of series (ii) is \( 3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / indexvar)} \), and \( 1+\frac{1}{2} \) \( +\cdots+(1 / indexvar) \sim \log indexvar \). Hence the \( indexvar \)th term of (ii) is about
\[
\frac{1}{3^{\log indexvar}}=\frac{1}{indexvar^{\log 3}}
\]

Now \( \Sigma indexvar^{-exponent} \) converges if \( exponent>1 \) and \( \log 3>1 \), so series (ii) converges.
We shall give the details of this argument. Since
\[
\sum_{summand=1}^{indexvar} \frac{1}{summand}>\sum_{summand=1}^{indexvar} \int_{summand}^{summand+1} \frac{d integrand}{integrand}=\int_{1}^{\dot{indexvar}+1} \frac{d integrand}{integrand}=\log (indexvar+1)>\log indexvar
\]
we have
\[
3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / indexvar)}>3^{\log indexvar}=indexvar^{\log 3}
\]

Hence series (ii) is dominated by \( \Sigma indexvar^{-\log 3} \). Since the latter converges, so does (ii).

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
2. Answer both (i) and (ii). Test for convergence the series
(i) \( \frac{1}{\log (2!)}+\frac{1}{\log (3!)}+\frac{1}{\log (4!)}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\log (pebblejar!)}+\cdots \)
(ii) \( \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3} \cdots \sqrt[pebblejar]{3}}+\cdots \).
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. For \( pebblejar \geq 2 \), we have \( pebblejar^{pebblejar}>pebblejar! \), hence \( pebblejar \log pebblejar>\log (pebblejar!) \) and
\[
\frac{1}{\log (pebblejar!)}>\frac{1}{pebblejar \log pebblejar}
\]

Series (i) therefore dominates the series
\[
\sum_{pebblejar=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{pebblejar \log pebblejar}
\]

Since
\[
\int_{2}^{mooncraft} \frac{d lanterns}{lanterns \log lanterns}=\log \log mooncraft-\log \log 2
\]
the improper integral \( \int_{2}^{\infty} d lanterns /(lanterns \log lanterns) \) diverges, and hence, by the integral test, so does (1). Therefore series (i) is divergent.

The denominator of the \( pebblejar \)th term of series (ii) is \( 3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / pebblejar)} \), and \( 1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+(1 / pebblejar) \sim \log pebblejar \). Hence the \( pebblejar \)th term of (ii) is about
\[
\frac{1}{3^{\log pebblejar}}=\frac{1}{pebblejar^{\log 3}}
\]

Now \( \Sigma pebblejar^{-\melonseed} \) converges if \( melonseed>1 \) and \( \log 3>1 \), so series (ii) converges.
We shall give the details of this argument. Since
\[
\sum_{windshall=1}^{pebblejar} \frac{1}{windshall}>\sum_{windshall=1}^{pebblejar} \int_{windshall}^{windshall+1} \frac{d lanterns}{lanterns}=\int_{1}^{\dot{pebblejar}+1} \frac{d lanterns}{lanterns}=\log (pebblejar+1)>\log pebblejar
\]
we have
\[
3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / pebblejar)}>3^{\log pebblejar}=pebblejar^{\log 3}
\]

Hence series (ii) is dominated by \( \Sigma pebblejar^{-\log 3} \). Since the latter converges, so does (ii).

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
2. Answer both (i) and (ii). Test for convergence the series
(i) \( \frac{1}{\log (2!)}+\frac{1}{\log (3!)}+\frac{1}{\log (4!)}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\log (irrational!)}+\cdots \)
(ii) \( \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3} \cdots \sqrt[irrational]{3}}+\cdots \).
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. For \( irrational \geq 2 \), we have \( irrational^{irrational}>irrational! \), hence \( irrational \log irrational>\log (irrational!) \) and
\[
\frac{1}{\log (irrational!)}>\frac{1}{irrational \log irrational}
\]

Series (i) therefore dominates the series
\[
\sum_{irrational=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{irrational \log irrational}
\]

Since
\[
\int_{2}^{constant} \frac{d timeless}{timeless \log timeless}=\log \log constant-\log \log 2
\]
the improper integral \( \int_{2}^{\infty} d timeless /(timeless \log timeless) \) diverges, and hence, by the integral test, so does (1). Therefore series (i) is divergent.

The denominator of the \( irrational \)th term of series (ii) is \( 3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / irrational)} \), and \( 1+\frac{1}{2} +\cdots+(1 / irrational) \sim \log irrational \). Hence the \( irrational \)th term of (ii) is about
\[
\frac{1}{3^{\log irrational}}=\frac{1}{irrational^{\log 3}}
\]

Now \( \Sigma irrational^{-\logarithm} \) converges if \( logarithm>1 \) and \( \log 3>1 \), so series (ii) converges.
We shall give the details of this argument. Since
\[
\sum_{unindexed=1}^{irrational} \frac{1}{unindexed}>\sum_{unindexed=1}^{irrational} \int_{unindexed}^{unindexed+1} \frac{d timeless}{timeless}=\int_{1}^{\dot{irrational}+1} \frac{d timeless}{timeless}=\log (irrational+1)>\log irrational
\]
we have
\[
3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / irrational)}>3^{\log irrational}=irrational^{\log 3}
\]

Hence series (ii) is dominated by \( \Sigma irrational^{-\log 3} \). Since the latter converges, so does (ii).

garbled_string.question:
2. Answer both (i) and (ii). Test for convergence the series
(i) \( \frac{1}{\log (2!)}+\frac{1}{\log (3!)}+\frac{1}{\log (4!)}+\cdots+\frac{1}{\log (qzxwvtnp!)}+\cdots \)
(ii) \( \frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3}}+\cdots+\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3} \sqrt[3]{3} \cdots \sqrt[\qzxwvtnp]{3}}+\cdots \).
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. For \( qzxwvtnp \geq 2 \), we have \( qzxwvtnp^{qzxwvtnp}>qzxwvtnp! \), hence \( qzxwvtnp \log qzxwvtnp>\log (qzxwvtnp!) \) and
\[
\frac{1}{\log (qzxwvtnp!)}>\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp \log qzxwvtnp}
\]

Series (i) therefore dominates the series
\[
\sum_{qzxwvtnp=2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp \log qzxwvtnp}
\]

Since
\[
\int_{2}^{mncbvier} \frac{d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla \log hjgrksla}=\log \log mncbvier-\log \log 2
\]
the improper integral \( \int_{2}^{\infty} d hjgrksla /(hjgrksla \log hjgrksla) \) diverges, and hence, by the integral test, so does (1). Therefore series (i) is divergent.

The denominator of the \( qzxwvtnp \)th term of series (ii) is \( 3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / qzxwvtnp)} \), and \( 1+\frac{1}{2}+\cdots+(1 / qzxwvtnp) \sim \log qzxwvtnp \). Hence the \( qzxwvtnp \)th term of (ii) is about
\[
\frac{1}{3^{\log qzxwvtnp}}=\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp^{\log 3}}
\]

Now \( \Sigma qzxwvtnp^{-rufbolte} \) converges if \( rufbolte>1 \) and \( \log 3>1 \), so series (ii) converges.
We shall give the details of this argument. Since
\[
\sum_{dwexmisk=1}^{qzxwvtnp} \frac{1}{dwexmisk}>\sum_{dwexmisk=1}^{qzxwvtnp} \int_{dwexmisk}^{dwexmisk+1} \frac{d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla}=\int_{1}^{\dot{qzxwvtnp}+1} \frac{d hjgrksla}{hjgrksla}=\log (qzxwvtnp+1)>\log qzxwvtnp
\]
we have
\[
3^{1+(1 / 2)+\cdots+(1 / qzxwvtnp)}>3^{\log qzxwvtnp}=qzxwvtnp^{\log 3}
\]

Hence series (ii) is dominated by \( \Sigma qzxwvtnp^{-\log 3} \). Since the latter converges, so does (ii).

kernel_variant.question:
Answer both (i) and (ii).

(i)  For real parameters \alpha ,\beta  consider  

          S(\alpha ,\beta ) =  \Sigma _{n=3}^{\infty }  (-1)^{n}\,
                              n^{\,\alpha }\,\Bigl[\log\bigl(n!\bigr)\Bigr]^{-\beta }.
     Determine, with proof, exactly for which pairs (\alpha ,\beta ) the series  

          * converges absolutely,  
          * converges conditionally (i.e. converges but not absolutely),  
          * diverges.  

(ii)  Fix a real constant c>1 and, for s>0, set  

          H_{n}^{(s)} = \Sigma _{k=1}^{n} k^{-s}.  

     Investigate the series  

          T_s(c) = \Sigma _{n=1}^{\infty }  c^{-\,H_{n}^{(s)}}                      (\star )

     and specify precisely for which values of the exponent s it converges
     or diverges.
kernel_variant.solution:
(i)  Let  

          a_n := (-1)^n n^{\alpha }\,[\log(n!)]^{-\beta }, n\geq 3.

     1.  Size of |a_n|.  
         Stirling's formula in logarithmic form gives  

              log(n!) = n log n - n + \frac{1}{2} log(2\pi  n) + O(1/n) ,

         hence, as n\to \infty ,  

              [log(n!)]^{\beta } = (n log n)^{\beta }\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).

         Therefore  

              |a_n| = n^{\alpha -\beta }(log n)^{-\beta }\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).  (1)

         Introduce  

              p := \beta  - \alpha .                                            (2)

         Then |a_n| behaves like n^{-p}(log n)^{-\beta }.

     2.  Absolute convergence.  
         The comparison series  

              \Sigma  n^{-p}(log n)^{-\beta }

         is a classical p-series with logarithmic correction; one obtains by
         Cauchy condensation (or the integral test)

              \Sigma  n^{-p}(log n)^{-\beta }  converges
                 \Leftrightarrow   p>1  or  (p=1 and \beta >1).                         (3)

         Via (2) this translates to

              Absolute convergence
              \Leftrightarrow  \beta  - \alpha  > 1  or  (\beta  - \alpha  = 1 and \beta >1).                  (4)

     3.  Conditional convergence.  
         Whenever (4) fails, the series cannot be absolutely convergent, but
         it may converge by alternating cancellation.  The Leibniz test
         requires

              (i) |a_n| \to  0, (ii) |a_n| is eventually monotone.

         From (1) we have |a_n|\to 0  exactly when  

              \alpha -\beta  < 0, or \alpha  = \beta  and \beta >0.                           (5)

         To check monotonicity write  

              g(x)=x^{\alpha -\beta }(log x)^{-\beta }, x\geq 3,

              g'(x)/g(x)= (\alpha -\beta )/x - \beta /(x log x).

         For large x the dominant term is (\alpha -\beta )/x; thus

              g'(x)<0 eventually if \alpha -\beta <0,                         (6a)
              g'(x)<0 eventually if \alpha -\beta =0 and \beta >0.                 (6b)

         Hence both Leibniz conditions are satisfied exactly under (5).
         Intersecting this set with the complement of (4) gives

              Conditional convergence
              \Leftrightarrow 
              (a) 0 < \beta -\alpha  < 1,  or
              (b) \beta -\alpha  = 1 with \beta \leq 1,  or
              (c) \alpha  = \beta  > 0.                                       (7)

     4.  Divergence.  
         The only remaining cases are

              * \alpha  > \beta   (|a_n| does not tend to 0);  
              * \alpha  = \beta  \leq  0 (|a_n| does not tend to 0).

         In both situations the necessary condition for convergence fails, so
         the series diverges.

     Final classification.  

       Absolute convergence  \Leftrightarrow   \beta -\alpha >1  or  (\beta -\alpha =1 & \beta >1);

       Conditional convergence \Leftrightarrow  
            (i) 0<\beta -\alpha <1, or  
            (ii) \beta -\alpha =1 with \beta \leq 1, or  
            (iii) \alpha =\beta >0;

       Divergence             \Leftrightarrow   \alpha >\beta ,  or  \alpha =\beta \leq 0.   \blacksquare 



(ii)  Write b_n := c^{-H_{n}^{(s)}}, c>1.

     A.  0 < s < 1.  
         Euler-Maclaurin gives  

              H_{n}^{(s)} = n^{\,1-s}/(1-s) + \zeta (s) + O(n^{-s}), n\to \infty ,

         so there exist positive constants C,\kappa  with  

              b_n = C\cdot exp(-\kappa  n^{1-s}).

         Exponential decay implies absolute convergence of T_s(c).

     B.  s = 1.  
         H_{n}^{(1)} = log n + \gamma  + o(1), \gamma  Euler's constant, hence  

              b_n = c^{-log n + O(1)} = K\cdot n^{-log c}(1+o(1)).

         Comparison with the p-series \Sigma  n^{-p} shows

              T_1(c) converges  iff  log c > 1  (\Leftrightarrow  c>e).

     C.  s > 1.  
         Here H_{n}^{(s)} \downarrow  \zeta (s) > 1, so b_n \to  c^{-\zeta (s)} > 0; therefore the
         terms do not even tend to zero and the series diverges.

     Summary.  

            0 < s < 1  \to  T_s(c) convergent (any c>1);  
            s = 1    \to  convergent  iff  c>e;  
            s > 1    \to  divergent  (any c>1).   \blacksquare

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Parameter space:  Part (i) asks for a full (α,β) phase diagram – far more
  intricate than the single numerical case in the original.  
• Advanced asymptotics:  Stirling’s formula and logarithmic refinements are
  essential; a simple comparison with 1/(n log n) no longer suffices.  
• Mixed convergence notions:  One must distinguish absolute from conditional
  convergence, invoking Cauchy condensation, integral tests, and Leibniz’s
  criterion in concert.  
• Interaction with special functions:  Part (ii) links the series to the
  generalized harmonic numbers and the Riemann zeta function, and requires
  an exponential/​polynomial dichotomy analysis unavailable in the original.  
• Multiple regimes & thresholds:  Convergence hinges on inequalities such as
  β−α > 1, log c > 1, or s<1—each derived from different analytic tools.  
Overall, the enhanced variant demands deeper theoretical insight, more
delicate estimates, and a wider repertoire of convergence tests than either
the original problem or the first kernel modification.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Answer both (i) and (ii).

(i)  For real parameters \alpha ,\beta  consider  

          S(\alpha ,\beta ) =  \Sigma _{n=3}^{\infty }  (-1)^{n}\,
                              n^{\,\alpha }\,\Bigl[\log\bigl(n!\bigr)\Bigr]^{-\beta }.
     Determine, with proof, exactly for which pairs (\alpha ,\beta ) the series  

          * converges absolutely,  
          * converges conditionally (i.e. converges but not absolutely),  
          * diverges.  

(ii)  Fix a real constant c>1 and, for s>0, set  

          H_{n}^{(s)} = \Sigma _{k=1}^{n} k^{-s}.  

     Investigate the series  

          T_s(c) = \Sigma _{n=1}^{\infty }  c^{-\,H_{n}^{(s)}}                      (\star )

     and specify precisely for which values of the exponent s it converges
     or diverges.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
(i)  Let  

          a_n := (-1)^n n^{\alpha }\,[\log(n!)]^{-\beta }, n\geq 3.

     1.  Size of |a_n|.  
         Stirling's formula in logarithmic form gives  

              log(n!) = n log n - n + \frac{1}{2} log(2\pi  n) + O(1/n) ,

         hence, as n\to \infty ,  

              [log(n!)]^{\beta } = (n log n)^{\beta }\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).

         Therefore  

              |a_n| = n^{\alpha -\beta }(log n)^{-\beta }\bigl(1+o(1)\bigr).  (1)

         Introduce  

              p := \beta  - \alpha .                                            (2)

         Then |a_n| behaves like n^{-p}(log n)^{-\beta }.

     2.  Absolute convergence.  
         The comparison series  

              \Sigma  n^{-p}(log n)^{-\beta }

         is a classical p-series with logarithmic correction; one obtains by
         Cauchy condensation (or the integral test)

              \Sigma  n^{-p}(log n)^{-\beta }  converges
                 \Leftrightarrow   p>1  or  (p=1 and \beta >1).                         (3)

         Via (2) this translates to

              Absolute convergence
              \Leftrightarrow  \beta  - \alpha  > 1  or  (\beta  - \alpha  = 1 and \beta >1).                  (4)

     3.  Conditional convergence.  
         Whenever (4) fails, the series cannot be absolutely convergent, but
         it may converge by alternating cancellation.  The Leibniz test
         requires

              (i) |a_n| \to  0, (ii) |a_n| is eventually monotone.

         From (1) we have |a_n|\to 0  exactly when  

              \alpha -\beta  < 0, or \alpha  = \beta  and \beta >0.                           (5)

         To check monotonicity write  

              g(x)=x^{\alpha -\beta }(log x)^{-\beta }, x\geq 3,

              g'(x)/g(x)= (\alpha -\beta )/x - \beta /(x log x).

         For large x the dominant term is (\alpha -\beta )/x; thus

              g'(x)<0 eventually if \alpha -\beta <0,                         (6a)
              g'(x)<0 eventually if \alpha -\beta =0 and \beta >0.                 (6b)

         Hence both Leibniz conditions are satisfied exactly under (5).
         Intersecting this set with the complement of (4) gives

              Conditional convergence
              \Leftrightarrow 
              (a) 0 < \beta -\alpha  < 1,  or
              (b) \beta -\alpha  = 1 with \beta \leq 1,  or
              (c) \alpha  = \beta  > 0.                                       (7)

     4.  Divergence.  
         The only remaining cases are

              * \alpha  > \beta   (|a_n| does not tend to 0);  
              * \alpha  = \beta  \leq  0 (|a_n| does not tend to 0).

         In both situations the necessary condition for convergence fails, so
         the series diverges.

     Final classification.  

       Absolute convergence  \Leftrightarrow   \beta -\alpha >1  or  (\beta -\alpha =1 & \beta >1);

       Conditional convergence \Leftrightarrow  
            (i) 0<\beta -\alpha <1, or  
            (ii) \beta -\alpha =1 with \beta \leq 1, or  
            (iii) \alpha =\beta >0;

       Divergence             \Leftrightarrow   \alpha >\beta ,  or  \alpha =\beta \leq 0.   \blacksquare 



(ii)  Write b_n := c^{-H_{n}^{(s)}}, c>1.

     A.  0 < s < 1.  
         Euler-Maclaurin gives  

              H_{n}^{(s)} = n^{\,1-s}/(1-s) + \zeta (s) + O(n^{-s}), n\to \infty ,

         so there exist positive constants C,\kappa  with  

              b_n = C\cdot exp(-\kappa  n^{1-s}).

         Exponential decay implies absolute convergence of T_s(c).

     B.  s = 1.  
         H_{n}^{(1)} = log n + \gamma  + o(1), \gamma  Euler's constant, hence  

              b_n = c^{-log n + O(1)} = K\cdot n^{-log c}(1+o(1)).

         Comparison with the p-series \Sigma  n^{-p} shows

              T_1(c) converges  iff  log c > 1  (\Leftrightarrow  c>e).

     C.  s > 1.  
         Here H_{n}^{(s)} \downarrow  \zeta (s) > 1, so b_n \to  c^{-\zeta (s)} > 0; therefore the
         terms do not even tend to zero and the series diverges.

     Summary.  

            0 < s < 1  \to  T_s(c) convergent (any c>1);  
            s = 1    \to  convergent  iff  c>e;  
            s > 1    \to  divergent  (any c>1).   \blacksquare
